title: Why Is My Cat's Ear Drooping? | Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Discover common causes for a drooping cat ear, such as hematomas or infections, and learn when to contact your vet in this triage guide.
content_html:
What is it?

A drooping or 'dropped' ear in a cat occurs when the ear flap (pinna) loses its upright position and folds downward. This is typically not a disease itself but a symptom of an underlying issue. Common causes include an aural hematoma—a pocket of blood that forms between the skin and cartilage—or severe inflammation from ear mites, bacterial infections, or polyps that make the ear too heavy or painful to hold up.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a drooping ear is generally Low. While it is not usually a life-threatening emergency, it is a sign of significant discomfort or irritation. You should monitor your cat for other symptoms like head tilting or loss of balance and schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. Delayed treatment can lead to permanent scarring and 'cauliflower ear' or the progression of a deep-seated infection.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

A clear photo of the affected ear is a vital tool for veterinary triage. It allows a professional to distinguish between a 'pillowy' swelling (indicative of a hematoma) and a flat, folded ear (suggestive of chronic infection or nerve issues). Visuals of the ear canal can also reveal discharge, redness, or debris, helping your vet determine if your cat needs immediate pain relief or specialized cleaning.

faqs.question: What is an aural hematoma?
faqs.answer: An aural hematoma is a collection of blood that pools inside the ear flap, usually caused by broken blood vessels from excessive head shaking or scratching.

faqs.question: Can ear mites cause a cat's ear to drop down?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ear mites cause intense itching and inflammation. The resulting head shaking can cause a hematoma, or the sheer amount of debris and swelling can cause the ear to fold.

faqs.question: Will my cat's ear ever stand up straight again?
faqs.answer: With prompt medical treatment, many ears return to normal. However, if left untreated, the cartilage may scar and shrink, leading to a permanently folded or crinkled appearance.
